dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a possible seizure in the city of [CITY].
Ma'am, I'm Janet with the rescue squad. Can you tell us what brought you to call 911 today?
She was walking when she had a seizure and fell down. We thought she hit her head, but she didn't say so.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name? And what day is today?
Her eyes are open. They appear to be reacting to light.
She answers questions, though she seems confused. She knows who she is, but not sure of the date.
She's trying to sit up, but starts to get agitated. She kicked the stretcher and punched the air a moment ago.
Is there anything in your mouth or throat that might be blocking your ability to speak?
Her respirations are steady. Are you having any trouble breathing right now?
Respiration rate is 20 per minute. She's breathing without obvious distress.
Her skin is warm and dry. Do you normally take any medications for high blood pressure or seizures?
Pulse is 102. It's strong and regular.
Blood pressure is 186 over 102. That's on the high side.
She has epilepsy. She usually has seizures sometimes. Her regular doctor is at [COMPANY_NAME].
Just walking on the sidewalk. She suddenly stiffened and fell to the ground.
I don't know exactly when she last ate, but she may have had coffee this morning.
Any pain after the seizure? If so, can you describe it?
On a scale from 0 to 10, 10 being the worst pain you've had, what number would you rate this?
We assessed her GCS at 14 - 4 on eyes, 4 on verbal, and 6 on motor movement.
Blood sugar is 118. She's fine with that.
Oxygen saturation is 100%. Good levels overall.
Her skin is clear with no rashes or discoloration. She looks warm and dry all over.
Eyes are PERRL, trachea midline, no jugular venous distention noted.
No nose bleed, throat breathing freely. No signs of trauma in the head.
Lungs are clear bilaterally. Heart sounds are regular and no chest discomfort is reported.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia. The rhythm is fast but regular.
We're going to apply a heart monitor so we can keep track of the EKG.
4-lead monitoring applied and working now.
We attempted to start a 20g IV on an extremity vein, but were unsuccessful due to her combative behavior.
IV access was not obtained after 1 attempt.
Her pulse is 108 now. Still fast but strong.
Breathing remains steady at 18 per minute.
Blood pressure is 169 over 88. A little lower now.
Blood sugar is not registering - the meter said zero. No signs of low sugar though.
Based on the vitals and her medical history, we're continuing with the seizure protocol, steps 3-12.
She was unconscious for a while after the seizure but is slowly returning to awareness.
Any change in pain? Still zero?
EKG still shows sinus tachycardia. Rhythm remained stable.
We reattempted an IV but she was still agitated. No airway compromise occurred during the attempt.
We are taking her to [COMPANY_NAME] since that's the hospital she routinely goes to.
Soft restraints applied due to her combative behavior. All seatbelts and straps are in place.
RN, we found that she has a history of epilepsy and hypertension. Had a seizure, was postictal, and combative in the field.
No IV access and no medication administered in the field.
Eyes stay open when not stimulated. Reaction is within normal limits.
She was able to move herself on the stretcher and into the hospital bed without help.
She was conscious when she arrived. Could not confirm ID or insurance due to no identification provided.
She has full motor function in all four extremities. No weakness or paralysis observed.
Sensory and motor function are normal in all limbs. She is able to grip and move with force.
No signs of cervical spine tenderness or stiffness.
Her skin is intact with no notable lesions or marks.
No known drug allergies reported.
She was taking medications for her seizures and high blood pressure.
Pulses in all four limbs are strong and equal.
No pain reported and she is more alert now.
We were friends of hers, and she always had a bag labeled with her medications at home.
Carotid pulses are strong and regular. No murmurs or rubs detected.
She was initially combative but settled after being placed in restraints.
We observed a deterioration in her blood pressure and oximetry during transport.
She remained seated upright in the stretcher and didn't require significant support.
Her oxygen saturation dropped slightly to 99%, but she remained breathing effortlessly.
We rechecked IV access but she remained combative, so no action was taken.
EKG rhythm remains unchanged. Sinus tach is ongoing.
Soft restraints were removed at the hospital upon nurse request.
Transport was uneventful after brief initial agitation.
We documented all vital signs, interventions, and reassessments.
She doesn't have any known cardiac history other than hypertension.
Blood pressure on arrival was 169/88. It was higher initially.
No signs of bite marks, bruises, or rashes seen.
No foreign bodies or obstructions identified in the airway.
Glucose was non-reactive on the meter. Quick physical was stable.
No neck masses or lymphadenopathy were palpable.
Respiration is 18 and easy. No signs of distress.
She cooperated post-restraint and was handed off without difficulty.
Chest expansion is symmetrical and equal bilaterally.
Continued on 100% oxygen, she's negated any need for additional oxygen.
Her behavior suggests no pain is currently present.
Her pulse remains 108. Sinus tach rhythm.
No changes in medication were seen before the episode.
She was moved into bed without assistance and was lucid mid-arrival.
She was alert and oriented x4 upon arrival.
No deformities or swelling found in extremities.
Sensation and movement remain fully intact in all limbs.
No acute myocardial infarction appeared in the EKG.
We had no difficulty confirming the level 1 trauma center's availability.
Cranial exam remained unremarkable with no signs of trauma.
No restraint breakage or airway compromise occurred during transport.
Breathing is rhythmic and consistent with pre-seizure levels.
No marked changes in vital signs occurred during the ride.
She has a history of hypertension.
Both systolic and diastolic pressures are within our monitored range.
No dysphonia or seating requirement throughout the mission.
She maintained heart rate and breathing stable during transport.
She was repositioned from stretcher to bed with no difficulty.
She was alert and oriented x4 upon arrival.
No change detected in pain levels.
Her skin has no signs of burns, lesions, or other skin pathology.
No signs of nasal obstruction or foul drainage.
She was verbally instructed to stay calm and reduce agitation.
The final EKG strip showed rhythm was still consistent.
She was taken to her usual health facility for continuity.
